name: Nimbarka
dates_notes: Sectarian dates much earlier (some traditions place him in the 11th c. or earlier); academic consensus 12th–13th c.
core_thesis: Nimbārka's *svābhāvika-bhedābheda* — also called *dvaitādvaita* in later doxography — holds that *bheda* and *abheda* are both intrinsic (*svābhāvika*) and equally primordial in the relation of Brahman to *cit* and *acit*: neither difference nor non-difference is reducible, derivative, or caused by any *upādhi*. Three reals are co-eternal: Brahman (identified with Kṛṣṇa, accompanied by Rādhā), the *cit* (sentient *jīvas*), and the *acit* (insentient nature). The *jīva* and world are *aṃśa* of Brahman: dependent, controlled, and non-different in essence yet really and inalienably distinct as the parts of a substantial whole. This refuses both Bhāskara's *aupādhika* qualification (Nimbārka's bheda is not adventitious) and any Advaitic dissolution of plurality at the limit. *Bhakti* to Kṛṣṇa, conceived as *prapatti* and *sakhya* within the Sanaka-sampradāya, is the operative soteriology; liberation is unending devotional participation in Brahman, not absorption that erases the *jīva*'s real distinctness.

engaged_works.title: Vedanta-Parijata-Saurabha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored Brahma-Sūtra commentary; the Sanaka-sampradāya's school-defining bhāṣya.
engaged_works.summary: Nimbārka's *Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* is a notably terse Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya — among the briefest of the major *sampradāya* commentaries — that nonetheless establishes the doctrinal core of *svābhāvika-bhedābheda*. Its method is to read each *sūtra* as licensing simultaneous and equally intrinsic *bheda* and *abheda* between Brahman and the threefold real of *cit*, *acit*, and *Īśvara*. The bhāṣya is identifiably Vaiṣṇava and Kṛṣṇa-centred: Brahman is named as Kṛṣṇa with Rādhā, and the consequent soteriology is *prapatti*-inflected *bhakti*. The compactness is doctrinal, not casual — it forces interpretation onto sub-commentary, particularly Śrīnivāsa's *Vedānta-Kaustubha*, which the Sanaka-sampradāya treats as the canonical reading. Polemically, the work positions itself against both *māyā-vāda* and the *aupādhika* qualification of Bhāskara, but does so largely by exegetical contrast rather than by extended *pūrvapakṣa-uttarapakṣa* construction. It is the school-defining text and remains the obligatory reference for Nimbārka-line Vedānta.

engaged_works.title: Dasha-Shloki
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored ten-verse summary of Nimbārka's doctrine.
engaged_works.summary: The *Daśa-Ślokī* (also called *Siddhānta-Ratna*) is Nimbārka's ten-verse doctrinal compendium and functions as the catechetical statement of *svābhāvika-bhedābheda*. In tightly compressed Sanskrit verse it names the three eternally real categories — *cit*, *acit*, and *Īśvara* (Kṛṣṇa with Rādhā) — and asserts their simultaneous, intrinsic difference and non-difference; it identifies *bhakti* and *prapatti* as the means of liberation; and it delineates the *jīva* as eternally an *aṃśa* of Brahman, never merging into and never being severed from its source. Because the *Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* is so terse, the *Daśa-Ślokī* serves as the school's portable doctrinal kernel — memorised, recited, and used as the structural template for later sub-commentaries by Śrīnivāsa, Keśava-Kāśmīrī, and the Sanaka-sampradāya tradition. It is the most direct first-person statement of Nimbārka's settled view available to readers.
